item.title: The MANIAC by Benjamin Labatut
item.description: A worthy successor to Labatut’s debut When We Cease to Understand the World, this is a three-part novel about Paul Ehrenfest, John von Neumann, and Lee Sedol’s match with AlphaGo.

item.title: Convenience Store Woman by Sayaka Murata
item.description: This book is simply about a person being true to herself. It’s not just that Keiko is a non-conformist; even most “non-conformists” find their own tribe.

item.title: Personal
item.description: Things on the internet that I like § I Don’t Need Anything From Here by László Krasznahorkai Meditations on Moloch by Scott Alexander Pitchfork review of Kid A1 On Chomsky and the Two Cultures of Statistical Learning by Peter Norvig 1993 Toni Morrison interview by Charlie Rose (especially around 38:00 to 40:30) Andrej Bauer’s photos of mathematicians Mac is Catholic, PC is Protestant by Umberto Eco Flannery O’Connor’s self-portrait2 Lena Zavaroni sings Going Nowhere and Ottessa Moshfegh interview where I found this video these versions of Terry Riley’s In C: Bang on a Can, “Mantra” by L’infonie3, Shanghai Film Orchestra4, In C Mali People of Programming Languages, interviews at POPL 2018 What Is Like To Be A Philosopher? interview series, especially those of Agnes Callard and David Chalmers Are you sure your name is not misspelled? I could’ve sworn it had an ‘a’ in it.

item.title: Home
item.description: I am a software engineer living in New York. In a previous life, I was a graduate student in the computer science department at Cornell University, studying under Andrew C.

item.title: Palo Alto by Malcolm Harris
item.description: This is a sprawling mess of a book that takes Palo Alto the city and uses it as a symbol of all the ills that capitalism has wrought. Harris takes the “Palo Alto System,” originally Leland Stanford’s innovative program for breeding thoroughbreds, as the original articulation of the ethos that pervades the companies revolving around the university which bears his late son’s name, as well as the students who study in its halls.

item.title: Ten Days That Shook the World by John Reed
item.description: The most important thing that I learned from this book is that the October Revolution was actually just a bunch of meetings.

item.title: Heaven by Mieko Kawakami
item.description: A plain, unassuming book that opens up into an intense meditation of Nietzschean themes in its second half. (See this review by Merve Emre for the same observation.

item.title: The Dispossessed by Ursula Le Guin
item.description: As always, Le Guin’s worldbuilding is impeccable here. The exploration of what an anarcho-syndicalist society would look like is thorough and realistic; indeed, it is an “ambiguous” utopia as some printings of the book make its subtitle to be.
